entries.title: Visible safeguard / refusal message
entries.user_sees: A message rendered by a product surface declining or redirecting the request, often stating that it cannot help with the request as phrased.
entries.api_correlate: The API-side correlate of this outcome is a successful (HTTP 200) response — not an HTTP error — with stop_reason 'refusal' and a stop_details object of type 'refusal'. stop_details may carry a documented category (cyber, bio, frontier_llm, reasoning_extraction) and a human-readable explanation; both are null when the decline maps to no named category. When the observation itself is that raw API response, classify it as evt-api-refusal-stop-reason per the observation-channel rule (events.md), not as this entry.
entries.can_conclude: That the request, as submitted, produced a decline the user could see on a product surface; the visible text is the evidence.
entries.cannot_conclude: Why the request was declined beyond any documented category shown, which specific classifier or rule applied, any threshold, or whether a rephrased request would fare differently. Do not infer internal moderation logic from the wording of the visible message.

entries.title: Visible model switch after a safeguard event
entries.user_sees: An indication that the response was produced by a different model than requested (e.g., a request intended for one model answered by Claude Opus 4.8), sometimes alongside or after a safeguard notice.
entries.api_correlate: On the API, a configured fallback surfaces as a content block of type 'fallback' with from/to model sub-objects; the response's top-level 'model' field names the serving model and usage.iterations records each attempt, and documented sticky routing can serve a later turn on the fallback model with no fallback block (clm-fallback-block). When the observation is the raw API response, classify it as evt-api-fallback-block per the observation-channel rule (events.md). Product surfaces document their own behavior separately: Claude Code re-runs a classifier-flagged request on the provider's default Opus model and shows a notice in the transcript (clm-claude-code-fallback); the Claude consumer apps switch automatically by default, show a notice, and label the response with the answering model (clm-webapp-switch-notice). The platform API page itself documents no consumer-surface behavior (clm-consumer-surface-fallback).
entries.can_conclude: That the user observed the response being attributed to a different model than the one requested — the switch they saw is a fact about their screen. On surfaces with documented behavior, the documented behavior may additionally be cited: in Claude Code, that a classifier-flagged request is re-run on the provider's default Opus model with a transcript notice and the session continues on that model (clm-claude-code-fallback); in the Claude consumer apps, that automatic switching is on by default, a notice is shown, the response is labeled with the answering model, and the user can switch back from the model picker (clm-webapp-switch-notice).
entries.cannot_conclude: The exact wording or appearance of any switch notice (not documented); classifier internals or thresholds; that a visible switch always means a safeguard decline — Claude Code also documents availability-based fallback model chains distinct from content-based fallback (clm-claude-code-fallback); or anything about a surface whose behavior no tracked official document describes — there, the switch remains the user's report.

entries.title: API stop_reason: refusal
entries.user_sees: A successful API response whose stop_reason is 'refusal', with a stop_details object of type 'refusal' that may carry a category and explanation.
entries.api_correlate: stop_reason 'refusal' on an HTTP 200 response; stop_details.category among the documented values (cyber, bio, frontier_llm, reasoning_extraction) or null, with explanation null in the same case. stop_details is null for every stop_reason other than 'refusal'; on Message Batches results it may be null even on a refusal, so detection checks stop_reason directly. When the blocked request had a billable cached prefix, stop_details may also carry a fallback_credit_token, redeemable on a fallback-model retry for cache-read pricing (clm-cookbook-fallback-billing).
entries.can_conclude: That the API declined this request on an otherwise successful response, and — when present — the documented category label and explanation. The documented follow-up is to read stop_details and optionally retry on a fallback model; a fallback_credit_token, when present, is the documented billing bridge for that retry.
entries.cannot_conclude: The classifier internals or thresholds behind a category; anything more from a null category than that the decline maps to no named category; or that resubmitting the same request must produce the same outcome.

entries.title: API fallback content block
entries.user_sees: A content block of type 'fallback' with from/to model fields; the response 'model' names the serving model.
entries.api_correlate: A content block of type 'fallback' with from/to model sub-objects marks each point where one model's output gives way to the next; the response's top-level 'model' field names the model that produced the returned message, and usage.iterations records every attempt (declining models as 'message' entries, the serving model as a 'fallback_message' entry).
entries.can_conclude: That a configured fallback served the response, which model produced the returned message, and where in the content the hand-off occurred.
entries.cannot_conclude: That fallback implies a safeguard decline in every case — fallback is a configured mechanism, and this taxonomy asserts no trigger beyond what the block itself records. That the absence of a fallback block proves the requested model served the turn — documented sticky routing can serve a fallback turn with no block; check usage.iterations. Or any internal routing detail beyond the documented fields.

entries.title: User-visible error
entries.user_sees: An error such as an authentication problem, permission denial, request-too-large, rate limit, or an overloaded/temporary-failure message.
entries.api_correlate: One of the documented HTTP error codes and their type strings (see clm-api-errors): 400 invalid_request_error, 401 authentication_error, 402 billing_error, 403 permission_error, 404 not_found_error, 409 conflict_error, 413 request_too_large, 429 rate_limit_error, 500 api_error, 504 timeout_error, 529 overloaded_error. Errors are returned as JSON with a top-level 'error' object carrying 'type' and 'message', plus a request_id.
entries.can_conclude: The documented meaning of the specific HTTP error code and type string the user saw, and — on the API — the documented error-object shape ('type', 'message', request_id).
entries.cannot_conclude: Provider-side capacity or account state beyond the documented meaning of the code. A truncated or cut-off response is not one of these errors: truncation surfaces on a successful response as stop_reason 'max_tokens' or 'model_context_window_exceeded' (clm-stop-reasons); do not conflate a stop condition with an HTTP error.

entries.title: No observable event
entries.user_sees: Nothing unusual; a pure prompt-quality review with no event reported.
entries.can_conclude: That the user is asking for prompt-quality help, not event explanation.
entries.cannot_conclude: Nothing event-related may be concluded or attached: with no reported event, the report carries no estimates layer, and findings are limited to prompt quality.
